analogy: Think of "Federalism" through the chapter's own opening images: 150 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Seven FEDERALISM INTRODUCTION Look at the political maps (on next two pages) of India 1947 and 2017. They have changed dramatically over the years. Boundaries of States have changed, names of States have changed, and the number of States... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Federalism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 150 Indian Constitution at Work Chapter Seven FEDERALISM INTRODUCTION Look at the political maps (on next two pages) of India 1947 and 2017.

The text explains that they have changed dramatically over the years.

The text explains that boundaries of States have changed, names of States have changed, and the number of States has changed.

The text explains that when India became independent, we had a number of provinces that the British government had organised only for administrative convenience.

The text explains that then a number of princely states merged with the newly independent Indian union.

The text explains that since then boundaries of States have been reorganised many times.

The text explains that during this entire period, not only did boundaries of States change, but in some cases, even their names changed according to the wishes of the people of those States.

The text explains that thus, Mysore changed to Karnataka and Madras became Tamil Nadu.

The text explains that the maps show these large scale changes that have taken place in the span of over seventy years.

The text explains that in a way, these maps also tell us the story of functioning of federalism in India.

The text explains that after studying this chapter you will be able to understand the following: ± what is Federalism; ± the federal provisions in the Indian Constitution; ± the issues involved in the relations between the centre and the States; and ± the special provisions for certain States having a distinct composition and historical features.

The text explains that 153 Chapter 7: Federalism WHAT IS FEDERALISM?

The text explains that uSSR was one of the world’s super powers, but after 1989 it simply broke up into several independent countries.

The text explains that one of the major reasons for its break up was the excessive centralisation and concentration of power, and the domination of Russia over other regions with independent languages and cultures of their own e.g.

The text explains that some other countries like Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, and Pakistan also had to face a division of the country.

The text explains that canada came very close to a break up between the English-speaking and the French-speaking regions of that country.

The text explains that each level of the polity has distinct powers and responsibilities and has a separate system of government. ± The details of this dual system of government are generally spelt out in a written constitution, which is considered to be supreme and which is also the source of the power of both sets of government.

The text explains that though democracy was restored in Nigeria in 1999, religious differences along with conflicts over who will control revenues from the oil resources continue to present problems before the Nigerian federation.

The text explains that local ethnic communities resist centralised control of the oil resources.

The text explains that the States have immense responsibilities but very meagre revenue sources.

The text explains that thus, the central government has many revenue sources and the States are mostly dependent on the grants and financial assistance from the centre.

The text explains that planning commission appointed by the union government is the coordinating machinery that controls and supervises the resources management of the States.

The text explains that this distribution of economic resources is considered lopsided and has led to charges of discrimination against States ruled by an opposition party. ± As you will study later, the Governor has certain powers to recommend dismissal of the State government and the dissolution of the Assembly.

The text explains that article 257 (1): The executive power of every State shall be so exercised as not to impede or prejudice the exercise of the executive power of the Union, and the executive power of the Union shall extend to the giving of such directions to a State as may appear to the Government of India to be necessary for that purpose. ± You have already studied in the chapter on executive that we have an integrated administrative system.

The text explains that in fact, these demands were a direct fallout of the fact that different parties were ruling at the centre and in many States.

The text explains that many States (Tamil Nadu, Punjab, West Bengal) and many parties (DMK, Akali Dal, CPI-M) have made demands of autonomy from time to time. ± Another demand is that States should have independent sources of revenue and greater control over the resources.

The text explains that interstate Conflicts While the States keep fighting with the centre over autonomy and other issues like the share in revenue resources, there have been many instances of disputes between two States or among more than two States.

The text explains that rivers are a major resource and therefore, disputes over river waters test the patience and cooperative spirit of the States.

The text explains that the Union government could not impose a financial emergency in the State and the Directive Principles did not apply in J&K.

The text explains that 173 Chapter 7: Federalism √ CU Status for Manipur University: Rajya Sabha on Wednesday passed a Bill to convert the Manipur University into a Central University with the Human Resource Development Minister promising such institutions in the North Eastern States of Arunachal Pradesh, Tripura and Sikkim as well. √ Funds released: The Centre has released Rs.

The text explains that state why. √ Federalism enhances the possibility of people from different regions to interact without the fear of one’s culture being imposed upon them by others. √ Federal system will hinder easier economic transaction between two different regions that have distinct types of resources. √ A federal system will ensure that the powers of those at the centre will remain limited.

The text explains that write an essay of not more than 300 words making your suggestions about: a. division of powers among the centre and the States, b. distribution of financial resources, c. methods of resolving inter-State disputes and d. appointment of Governors 5.

The text explains that why are many States unhappy about the role of the Governor?

The text explains that president’s rule can be imposed in a State if the government is not being run according to the provisions of the Constitution.

The text explains that state whether any of the following conditions are a fit case for imposition of President’s rule in the State.

The text explains that give reasons. √ two members of the State legislative assembly belonging to the main opposition party have been killed by criminals and the opposition is demanding dismissal of the State government. √ Kidnapping of young children for ransom is on rise.

The text explains that the number of crimes against women are increasing. √ No political party has secured majority in the recent elections of the State Legislative Assembly.

The text explains that it is feared that some MLAs from the other parties may be lured to support a political party in return for money. √ Different political parties are ruling in the State and at the centre and they are bitter opponents of each other. √ More than 2000 people have been killed in the communal riots. √ In the water dispute between the two States, one State government refused to follow the decision of the Supreme Court.

The text explains that what are the demands raised by States in their quest for greater autonomy?

The text explains that should some States be governed by special provisions?

The text explains that does this create resentment among other States?

The text explains that does this help in forging greater unity among the regions of the country?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 7: Federalism

schemes.point: It had a weak central government and the economy of each unit was independent. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch7 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat FEDERALISM and INTRODUCTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Federalism; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Federalism through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use FEDERALISM and INTRODUCTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
